arsenal: The only club to go an entire Premier League season unbeaten — 49 games without a loss.
Named after the Royal Arsenal munitions factory in Woolwich.
First London club to win the league title, back in 1931.
man_city: Won the treble in 2022/23 — Premier League, FA Cup, and Champions League.
Became the first English club to win four consecutive league titles in 2024.
Their home ground, the Etihad, sits on the site of the 2002 Commonwealth Games stadium.
man_utd: Sir Alex Ferguson managed the club for 26 years, winning 13 Premier League titles.
The Busby Babes — eight players died in the 1958 Munich air disaster.
First English club to win the European Cup, in 1968 under Matt Busby.
liverpool: Have won more European Cups/Champions Leagues than any other English club — six.
The Miracle of Istanbul — came back from 3-0 down at half time to win the 2005 CL final on penalties.
Bill Shankly built the foundation — he turned a Second Division club into European royalty.
chelsea: Roman Abramovich's takeover in 2003 transformed them from mid-table to serial champions.
Won the Champions League in 2012 beating Bayern in their own stadium.
Stamford Bridge has been their home since 1905 — one of the oldest grounds in England.
aston_villa: One of the founding members of the Football League in 1888.
Won the European Cup in 1982 — still one of England's greatest upsets.
Have won the league title seven times, the last in 1981.
spurs: First non-league club to win the FA Cup, back in 1901.
The only club to complete the league and FA Cup double in the 20th century, in 1961.
Their new stadium has a synthetic grass section that retracts to reveal an NFL field underneath.
newcastle: St James' Park is the third largest club stadium in England.
Alan Shearer — their all-time top scorer with 206 goals — turned down Manchester United to join them.
Saudi Arabian ownership arrived in 2021, beginning one of football's most dramatic club transformations.
brighton: Under Graham Potter and then Roberto De Zerbi became one of Europe's most watched tactical sides.
The Amex Stadium only opened in 2011 — before that they groundshared for over a decade.
Their data-driven approach to recruitment is considered among the best in world football.
brentford: Only club in England whose four adjacent sides of the ground are all on different streets.
Went 74 years without top-flight football before their 2021 Premier League promotion.
Ivan Toney served an eight-month betting ban — and came back scoring on his first game back.
fulham: Craven Cottage has been their home since 1896 — the only ground with a cottage inside it.
Their greatest ever season was reaching the Europa League final in 2010 under Roy Hodgson.
Yo-yo club by reputation but Craven Cottage's riverside setting makes them one of football's most charming clubs.
everton: Founding members of the Football League and have never been relegated from it — until 2024.
The Merseyside derby is the most played fixture in English top-flight history.
Their new stadium at Bramley-Moore Dock on the waterfront opened in 2025.
wolves: Were one of England's top clubs in the 1950s, winning three league titles in a decade.
Their famous gold and black kit has barely changed since the 1950s.
Reached back-to-back Europa League quarter-finals in 2019 and 2020.
west_ham: Known as the Academy of Football — Bobby Moore, Geoff Hurst, and Martin Peters all came through their ranks.
Three of England's 1966 World Cup winning goals were scored by West Ham players.
Moved to the London Stadium after the 2012 Olympics, leaving their beloved Upton Park.
crystal_palace: Named after the iconic Crystal Palace building from the 1851 Great Exhibition.
Ian Wright — one of Arsenal's greatest ever strikers — started his career at Palace.
Selhurst Park is one of the most intimidating atmospheres in the Premier League.
nott_forest: Won back-to-back European Cups in 1979 and 1980 under Brian Clough — a feat only five clubs have ever achieved.
Brian Clough spent just 44 days as Leeds manager before one of football's most legendary fallouts.
Were relegated in 1999 and spent 23 years outside the top flight before returning in 2022.
bournemouth: Were in the fourth tier as recently as 2009 — five promotions in seven years took them to the Premier League.
Eddie Howe built the foundation that turned a near-bankrupt club into a top-flight side.
Their Vitality Stadium holds just 11,307 — the smallest in the Premier League.
sunderland: Won six league titles before World War II — one of England's most decorated pre-war clubs.
The Sunderland 'Til I Die documentary on Netflix became one of football's most watched series.
Stadium of Light replaced Roker Park in 1997 — built on the site of a former colliery.
leeds: Don Revie's Leeds of the late 60s and early 70s were one of Europe's most feared sides.
Spent 16 years outside the Premier League after financial collapse in the early 2000s.
Marcelo Bielsa's two seasons transformed them from a mid-Championship club to promotion winners playing some of England's most exciting football.
burnley: Won the league title in 1960 with a squad costing less than £30,000.
One of only four clubs to have played in all four divisions of the Football League.
Sean Dyche kept them in the Premier League for six consecutive seasons on one of the division's smallest budgets.
